footer.text: MIT © bankai-vue · This site is built with bankai-vue.

hero.title: The agnostic, accessibility-first Vue 3 component framework
hero.lede: Guiding defaults you can fully restyle · bring your own CSS framework · native modern HTML · first-class TypeScript · MIT, forever.

home.earlyNote: Early development — the API is being designed in the open. Nothing is on npm yet.

componentsIndex.heading: Components
componentsIndex.lede: The components built so far. More land as the roadmap progresses — this list grows with them.

getting-started.name: Getting started
getting-started.tagline: Install, add a theme, and render your first component.

rendering.name: SSR, SPA & routing
rendering.tagline: Hydration model, router auto-detection, and linkOrigin.

i18n.name: Internationalization
i18n.tagline: Localize default strings with locale bundles.

layout.tagline: App shell emitting native landmark regions.

gettingStarted.heading: Getting started
gettingStarted.lede: Install {core}, add a theme for the CSS, and render your first component. Works the same in a plain Vue (Vite) app or in Nuxt.

callout.body: The API is being designed in the open and nothing is on npm yet — the steps below document the intended shape. Follow the {roadmap} for the first release.

install.heading: Installation
install.body: You need two packages: {core} for the components and a theme for the CSS. Core ships {no} CSS of its own — without a theme the components render, but unstyled.

vite.heading: Vue (Vite)
vite.intro: Install the plugin with {createBankai} and import the theme's CSS once at your entry point.
vite.then: Then import components where you use them:

nuxt.heading: Nuxt
nuxt.intro: Add the first-party module and the theme CSS. The module auto-registers every component (so templates use {tag} with no import), auto-imports the composables, and installs the config per app so it stays per-request under SSR.
nuxt.optionsIntro: Configure the module under the {bankai} key. Every option is optional.

theme.heading: Choosing a theme
theme.intro: Themes are separate CSS packages — core resolves against none of them, so you pick one and import its CSS. Two ship today:
theme.bankai: {pkg} — the signature house look, an opinionated set of styled defaults. Customize it through {vars} custom properties.
theme.tailwind: {pkg} — remaps the design tokens onto your Tailwind scale, so your own Tailwind design language drives the components. Requires Tailwind CSS v4.
theme.override: Both override cleanly because the theme CSS is authored with zero-specificity {where} selectors.

config.heading: Configuration
config.intro: Pass initial config to {createBankai} (or the module's {bankaiConfig}). Read or mutate it at runtime with {useBankaiConfig} inside a component's setup.

options.idGeneration: Whether components generate a stable id when the consumer supplies none. A consumer-provided id always wins.
options.warnings: Emit development warnings for likely-mistaken usage. Already stripped from production builds; set false to silence them in dev too.
options.linkComponent: Force the component BankaiLink renders for internal navigation. Leave unset to auto-detect NuxtLink → RouterLink → <a>.
options.linkOrigin: Site origin BankaiLink compares an href against to decide it is external. Set it for accurate, hydration-safe checks under SSR/SSG.
options.linkNoopener: Auto-add rel="noopener noreferrer" to a target="_blank" link. A consumer-provided rel always wins.
options.codeBlockCopiedDuration: How long (ms) BankaiCodeBlock's copy button stays in its copied state before reverting. A per-instance copiedDuration prop overrides it.

next.heading: Next steps
next.body: Browse the {components} for props, slots, and live examples.

app.lede: The infra singleton at the root of an application — the outermost layer of App › Layout › Page › Container. It renders a plain {div} and is {not} a landmark.
app.usageBody: Wrap your application once at the root. It is a {singleton}: its services are provided by injection, so nesting an App inside another App would silently shadow the outer one — {nesting}. Placing Apps {sideBySide} is legitimate (embedded micro-frontends, split-screen).
app.embeddedBody: Its one job today is a {surface}: the theme carries {colorScheme} and the foundation {bg}/{fg} tokens on the App's {own} box. So a bankai island dropped into a foreign page paints the house light/dark surface for its subtree {without} the global {html} page paint (which an embedded consumer can sever). The box below sits on a non-house background to show its own painted surface:
app.override: Every theme rule is zero-specificity ({where}), so a plain declaration or utility class overrides it without {important}.
app.thinBody: {bankaiApp} takes no props today. Its richer infra role — a single overlay/portal mount target, a toast host and an app-config context — arrives with the overlay foundation, when the overlay root it would provide first has consumers. Until then it ships only the surface, rather than a speculative empty API.

aside.lede: A standalone {aside} complementary region — a side rail beside the main content, typically a sidebar nav. The native element carries the {complementary} role; the theme paints the house side-rail look (padding and an inline-end divider). Reach for it when you build a shell {without} {bankaiLayout}.
aside.namingBody: A page can hold more than one {complementary} landmark (a left nav and a right rail, say). When it does, each needs an accessible name so a screen reader's landmark list can tell them apart — pass {label}, which sets {ariaLabel} (e.g. {ex1}, {ex2}). A single, unambiguous aside can omit it. Your own {ariaLabel2} / {ariaLabelledby} takes precedence over the prop, so you can point at a visible heading instead.
aside.uniquenessBody: Do {not} place it inside {bankaiLayout}'s {sidebar} slot: {bankaiLayout2} already emits an {aside} for that slot, so a nested {bankaiAside} would produce an {aside2} inside an {aside3}. With {bankaiLayout3}, drop your sidebar straight into {sidebar2}; use {bankaiAside2} only for a hand-rolled shell or a second rail. Every theme rule is zero-specificity ({where}), so a plain declaration or utility class overrides the padding, divider, or background without {important}.

button.lede: A native {button} with typed variant, size, type, and disabled props. Ships no CSS of its own — the theme styles the {cls} class and {state} state.

code.lede: A minimal inline code primitive: a native {code} with a monospace font and a subtle chip background. Reach for it for an identifier, token, path, or short snippet inside a sentence — the block, fenced variant is a separate {codeBlock} (on the roadmap).
code.exampleP2: The size is {em}-relative, so a snippet like {token} stays proportional even in this smaller line.
code.semanticsBody: It renders a real {code}, so the phrasing semantics live on the element itself — assistive tech and the browser treat it as code for free. Today it takes no props; it is a pure, themeable wrapper. A polymorphic {as} (to reach the sibling monospace elements {kbd}, {samp}, {var}) may arrive later if dogfooding calls for it — a non-breaking addition.
code.themingBody: Every theme rule is zero-specificity ({where}), so a single declaration or a utility class overrides the look without {important}. Retune the chip by overriding a token — globally on {root}, or locally on any ancestor.

tokens.bg: The inline chip background (house default: the surface role).
tokens.fontSize: The chip font-size, em-relative so it scales with the surrounding text.
tokens.fontMono: The shared monospace stack --bankai-code-font-family defaults to (also feeds BankaiCodeBlock).

codeBlock.lede: A block code primitive: a native {preCode} with a copy-to-clipboard button. For an identifier or short snippet inside a sentence, reach for the inline {code} instead.
codeBlock.exampleBody: Pass the snippet as the {code} prop — it is both what renders and the exact string the copy button writes. Click {copy} to try it.
codeBlock.byoBody: Core highlights nothing — it stays design- and tooling-agnostic. The {language} prop only reflects as a {langClass} class on the {code}, the de-facto hook a highlighter (Shiki, Prism, highlight.js) or your own CSS keys off. To render already-highlighted markup, pass it through the default slot; the {codeProp} prop stays the exact string the copy button writes, so the copied text is never the highlighter's wrapper markup.
codeBlock.a11yBody1: The copy button is a composed {bankaiButton} whose accessible name tracks its visible label; a successful copy is announced (and re-announced on a repeat copy) through a visually hidden {status} live region. Override the {copyLabel} / {copiedLabel} props to set both the button text and the announcement for one block, use the {copySlot} slot (which receives the {copied} state) to swap in an icon, or set {copyableFalse} to drop the button entirely. The copied state's duration is tunable per-block via {copiedDuration} (ms) and globally via {config} (default {default}).
codeBlock.a11yBody2: To localize the labels {appWide} rather than per block, set a locale in the i18n config instead of the props — see the {guideLink}. A per-block prop always wins over the configured locale.
codeBlock.themingBody: Every theme rule is zero-specificity ({where}), so a single declaration or a utility class overrides the look without {important}. Retune the panel by overriding a token — globally on {root}, or locally on any ancestor.

tokens.bg: The panel background (house default: the surface role).
tokens.paddingBlock: Vertical padding of the scrolling panel.
tokens.paddingInline: Horizontal padding of the scrolling panel.
tokens.copyOffset: Inset of the copy button from the top inline-end corner.

container.lede: A width utility: centered at a themeable max-width by default, edge-to-edge with {fluid}. Reusable anywhere — a Card, a section, a hero — not once per route. The width layer of App › Layout › Page › Container; it is not a landmark and never renders its own {main}.
container.responsiveHeading: Responsive by available space, not the viewport
container.responsiveBody: The centered width is intrinsic: {maxInlineSize} resolves against the containing block, and {marginInline} centers within it. So a container caps at its max-width when there is room (bars on the sides) and {collapses} when its parent is narrower — with no media queries. This is deliberate: it stays correct inside an embedded or split-screen pane, where the viewport is wide but the container's box is only a fraction of it — the case a viewport breakpoint gets wrong. {fluid} is for the opposite intent: fill the width {evenWhen} there is room for bars (full-bleed heroes, dashboards). A full-bleed hero over a centered body is just two containers at different widths.
container.responsiveCollapses: collapses to edge-to-edge on its own
container.themingBody: The cap and gutter are custom properties, so you retune the width by overriding one property (no selector needed) — globally on {root}, or locally on any ancestor.

tokens.maxWidth: The centered content cap (house default 80rem). Ignored when fluid.
tokens.gutter: Inline padding so content never kisses the edge when the box is edge-to-edge.

flex.lede: A polymorphic flexbox container. Props reflect as {data} and {gap} rides a custom property, styled by zero-specificity {where} theme rules — so a consumer's utility classes override without {important}.

footer.lede: A standalone {footer} contentinfo region — the foot of the page (copyright, secondary links, site meta). The native element carries the {contentinfo} role; the theme paints the house foot look, the mirror of {bankaiHeader} (padding and a top border). Reach for it when you build a shell {without} {bankaiLayout}.
footer.uniquenessBody1: A native {footer} is the {contentinfo} landmark only when it is {not} nested inside {article}, {aside}, {main}, {nav}, or {section} — so use {bankaiFooter} at the top level of your document, not deep inside content.
footer.uniquenessBody2: Do {not} place it inside {bankaiLayout}'s {footerSlot} slot: {bankaiLayout2} already emits a {footer} for that slot, so a nested {bankaiFooter} would produce a {footer2} inside a {footer3} — a duplicate contentinfo landmark. With {bankaiLayout3}, drop your footer content straight into {footerSlot2}; use {bankaiFooter2} only for a hand-rolled shell. Every theme rule is zero-specificity ({where}), so a plain declaration or utility class overrides the padding, border, or background without {important}.

grid.lede: A polymorphic CSS-grid container for 2D layouts. Enumerated props reflect as {data}; continuous track values ({tracks}) ride {vars} custom properties read by the theme's {where} rules.

header.lede: A standalone {header} banner region — the top-of-page landmark that typically holds your navbar. The native element carries the {banner} role; the theme paints the house bar look (padding and a bottom border). Reach for it when you build a shell {without} {bankaiLayout}.
header.uniquenessBody1: A native {header} is the {banner} landmark only when it is {not} nested inside {article}, {aside}, {main}, {nav}, or {section} — so use {bankaiHeader} at the top level of your document, not deep inside content.
header.uniquenessBody2: Do {not} place it inside {bankaiLayout}'s {headerSlot} slot: {bankaiLayout2} already emits a {header} for that slot, so a nested {bankaiHeader} would produce a {header2} inside a {header3} — a duplicate banner landmark. With {bankaiLayout3}, drop your navbar straight into {headerSlot2}; use {bankaiHeader2} only for a hand-rolled shell.
header.stickyBody: The theme paints the page background onto the header's own box, so a {sticky} bar stays opaque over content scrolling beneath it — no extra background needed. Every theme rule is zero-specificity ({where}), so a plain declaration or utility class overrides the padding, border, or background without {important}.

heading.lede: A native {h1}–{h6} chosen by a required {level}. The element itself carries the document-outline semantics that assistive tech navigates by, so the level is always an explicit, deliberate choice — never a default.
heading.scaleBody: Each level maps to a size step the theme paints (keyed on {dataLevel}). Headings ship {margin} — spacing is the layout's job ({flex} / {stack} {gap}).
heading.requiredHeading: Level is required — and it means the outline
heading.requiredBody1: There is no universally-safe default heading level, and a wrong one silently breaks the document outline screen-reader users navigate by — so {level} has no default and must be stated. Pick it by the heading's place in the page hierarchy (don't skip levels), not by how big you want it to look.
heading.requiredBody2: Decoupling the visual size from the semantic level (an {h2} styled like an {h1}, the proper fix for wanting a size without skipping a level) is planned as a future {size} prop; today the visual size tracks the level.
heading.themingBody: Every step is a {vars} custom property (declared on {root}), so you retune one size, its line height, the shared weight, or the color by overriding a single property — no selector, no {important}. For example, override {example} to resize the level-1 step alone.

layout.lede: The persistent app shell: a CSS-grid root that wraps each slot in its native landmark region ({regions}), so an application gets a correct, unique-per-page landmark set for free. The grid tracks are yours to control with plain CSS against {cls} — there is no {view}-string DSL.
layout.structureBody: The default theme shell — header across the top, footer across the bottom, sidebar left of the main content. Absent regions collapse their track, so a header-less or sidebar-less app needs no override.
layout.customizingBody: {bankaiLayout} takes no layout props — you own the grid tracks with plain CSS. Every theme rule is zero-specificity ({where}), so a single declaration or utility class overrides it without {important}. Regions are pinned by name ({gridArea}, …), so restructuring is just a new {gridTemplateAreas} on {cls} — the DOM/source order stays {order}, only the visual placement moves.
layout.rtlBody: Right-to-left works with no extra code. The default uses logical values ({cols}, {minBlockSize}) and named areas rather than physical left/right, and CSS Grid lays columns along the inline axis — so under {rtl} the sidebar moves to the inline-start (right) edge automatically.
layout.scrollBody: By default {sidebar} and {main} stretch to fill the space between header and footer (the middle row is {fr}), and the whole page scrolls — the footer scrolls in after the content, but stays pinned to the bottom of the viewport on short pages ({minBlockSize}). For an app-shell feel — fixed header and footer with only {main2} scrolling — pin the grid to the viewport and make {main3} the scroll container (no {positionFixed} needed); a sticky header is a one-liner. To let the sidebar hug its content instead of stretching, use {alignSelf}.
layout.tailwindBody: Utilities win by ordinary specificity: {colSpan}/{rowSpan} on children and {gridCols}/{gridRows} on the root all override the theme. One caveat — the theme also sets {gridTemplateAreas}, which governs where the named regions land and implies its own column count. Overriding only {gridTemplateColumns} (e.g. {gridCols3}) while the areas remain leaves a stray empty track and the regions still pinned to their areas; to go fully line-based, also clear the template with {areasNone}.

link.lede: A router-aware link primitive. With no wiring it renders a native {a}; under vue-router or Nuxt it resolves the globally-registered {routerLink} / {nuxtLink} for internal {to} navigation — so one component works across plain Vue, vue-router, and Nuxt.
link.routerBody: Resolution order is {nuxtLink} → {routerLink} → native {a}, detected from the app's globally-registered components — so core needs no dependency on vue-router. Override the internal-link component with {linkComponent}. For first-class vue-router typing of {to}, add {types} to your tsconfig {typesKey}. A {targetBlank} link gets {rel} automatically (opt out via {linkNoopener}). An {href} to a different host reflects {external}; set {linkOrigin} to your site origin so that check is accurate under SSR/SSG.

main.lede: A standalone {main} content region — the document's primary content. The native element carries the {mainRole} role. Reach for it when you build a shell {without} {bankaiLayout}.
main.oneBody: The {mainRole} landmark must be {unique} — a page has exactly one {main}, so {bankaiMain} takes no accessible-name prop (unlike {bankaiAside}, of which there can be several). Do {not} place it inside a {bankaiLayout}: Layout's default slot already emits the sole {main2}, so a nested {bankaiMain2} would be a {main3} inside a {main4}.
main.paintsBody: Almost nothing — it {is} the page surface (no background), and content width and gutter belong to {bankaiContainer} (no padding). The one house default is a {minInlineSize} floor: as a grid or flex child the region would otherwise floor at its content's min width, so a wide, unbreakable descendant (a long {pre}, a table) pushes the whole content column wider and causes horizontal overflow. Flooring at {zero} lets the region shrink to its container and the descendant scroll or wrap within. Every theme rule is zero-specificity ({where}), so a plain declaration or utility class overrides it without {important}.

page.lede: The per-route content host — the box at the top of every route file. It sits inside the document's {main} and hosts a route's content; it is {not} a landmark of its own.
page.usageBody: The nesting is App › Layout › {page} › Container: {bankaiLayout} (or a standalone {bankaiMain}) emits the {main}, {bankaiPage} hosts the route inside it, and {bankaiContainer} sets the content width.
page.fillsBody: Its one house default is a {minBlockSize} fill so a short route still occupies the full content region and pushes a footer to the bottom. The fill is {percent} of the containing block, {not} a {dvh} viewport unit — a viewport height would break embedded or side-by-side panes where the box is a fraction of the window (it fills whatever space the parent gives it). Every theme rule is zero-specificity ({where}), so a plain declaration or utility class overrides it without {important}.
page.notLandmarkHeading: Not a landmark, no black magic
page.notLandmarkBody: {bankaiPage} renders a plain {div} — it is deliberately not a landmark and never renders its own {main} (Layout's default slot already emits the sole one). It also does {no} implicit child-rewriting: there is no auto heading-level context, so a {bankaiHeading}'s tag is always explicit via its {level}. It lands thin today; per-route scroll and route-transition concerns arrive once there is routing to dogfood.

text.lede: A polymorphic text primitive. Pick the element with {as} (including inline semantics like {code}/{kbd}/{mark}) and style with {size}/{weight}/{tone} — each a named set plus a verbatim escape hatch.

renderingPage.lede: bankai-vue components behave the same in a client-only Vue app (SPA) and under Nuxt server rendering (SSR) or static generation (SSG). This page covers what makes that work — the hydration model, how {bankaiLink} finds your router, and the one config value you should set when you server-render.
renderingPage.environmentsSpa: {spa} Install the plugin with {createBankai} and import components where you use them. Everything runs on the client.
renderingPage.environmentsNuxt: {nuxtSsr} The {module} auto-registers every component and installs the config {perApp} via a generated plugin — so under SSR each request gets its own reactive config, with no cross-request leakage.
renderingPage.hydrationBody1: Components are SSR-safe by design: they generate hydration-stable ids (Vue's {useId}) and read no {window} or {document} during setup, so the server HTML and the client's first render always agree.
renderingPage.hydrationBody2: Any state that can only be known on the client is deliberately withheld until {after} hydration, then updates reactively. The clearest example is {bankaiLink}'s {external} flag: on the server and the first client render it is computed from {linkOrigin} (or an origin-less fallback); the accurate {windowLocation}-based host check only kicks in once the component has mounted. That is why the flag never causes a hydration mismatch.
renderingPage.routerIntro: {bankaiLink} renders the right element for internal ({to}) navigation with no wiring, resolved in this order:
renderingPage.routerItem1: an explicit {linkComponent} override in {bankaiConfig}, if you set one;
renderingPage.routerItem2: a globally-registered {nuxtLink} (preferred under Nuxt — it adds prefetch, base-URL, and external-URL handling);
renderingPage.routerItem4: otherwise a native {a} — no router installed.
renderingPage.routerBody: Detection reads the app's global component registry, which is exactly where {vueRouter} and Nuxt register these — so core never imports a router and stays dependency-free. A {string} {to} with no router degrades to a plain {aHref}.
renderingPage.typesBody: By default {to} is a router-agnostic type. If you use vue-router, add the types entry to your {tsconfig} to type it as vue-router's {routeLocationRaw}. It is an opt-in augmentation, so a router-free app stays dependency-free.
renderingPage.originBody1: To decide a link is external, {bankaiLink} compares its {href} host against a reference origin. On the server there is no {window} — and under SSG there is no request at all — so the current origin is not knowable at render time.
renderingPage.originBody2: Set {linkOrigin} to your canonical site origin so the check is accurate and identical on server and client. A client-only SPA can leave it unset — it falls back to {windowLocation} after hydration; with no origin available at all, any absolute {httpS} URL is treated as external.
renderingPage.seeAlsoBody: {gettingStarted} for install and configuration, and {link} for its full props and reflected state.

i18nPage.lede: Components ship English default strings — a copy button's {copy}, and more as components grow. bankai-vue localizes them through one small config surface, so you set a locale once and every component's defaults follow. Per-instance props still override a single element.
i18nPage.registerBody1: Locale bundles are opt-in, tree-shakeable exports. Import the ones you use, register them under {messages}, and set {locale}. A German ({de}) bundle ships today; English is the built-in base and needs no import.
i18nPage.registerBody2: Under Nuxt it is even less: set {locale} and the module auto-injects the matching built-in bundle into its generated plugin — no import, no {messages} — as a static, tree-shaken, SSR-safe import. Register a bundle in {messages2} only to override it or add a locale core doesn't ship. The config is installed per app, so it stays per-request under SSR.
i18nPage.coverageBody: English is the complete base — 100% by definition. Each shipped bundle covers a share of the message keys; whatever it omits falls through to English. As new components add default strings, a bundle's coverage drops until the new keys are translated.
i18nPage.contributeBody: Locale bundles are small partial dictionaries — completing a bundle below 100%, or adding a language bankai-vue doesn't ship yet, is a great first contribution. Each is a plain object of message keys with English as the fallback, so you translate only what you want.
i18nPage.contributeLink: Browse the locale bundles on GitHub →
i18nPage.overrideBody: A bundle can be {partial}. Any key you leave out falls through to the complete English base, so you can register a shipped bundle, hand-write a bundle for a locale that has none yet, or tweak a single string — all through the same {messages} registry.
i18nPage.regionalBody: A regional locale inherits its base language automatically: {deAt} resolves every {de} string, and only the keys you give {deAt2} override it. Set {fallbackLocale} for a cross-family fallback (tried before the English base). The full chain is: active locale and its regional parents → {fallbackLocale2} and its parents → English.
i18nPage.precedenceIntro: Each default string resolves in this order:
i18nPage.runtimeBody: The config is reactive, so assigning a new {locale} re-renders every component's labels — no reload, no re-mount.
i18nPage.seeAlsoBody: {link} — the first component with localizable strings, and its {copyLabel} / {copiedLabel} props.
